technology.description: Azure Red Hat OpenShift is a Preview family with 52 normalized items, 15 high-severity findings, covers Identity and Access Management, Network topology and connectivity, Operations management, and uses generalized guidance.
technology.whatThisMeans: Use to enrich design reviews, but validate recommendations before treating them as baseline controls.

quality.label: Preview · Medium confidence
quality.summary: Use with architectural judgment. Preview content is valuable, but it should not be treated as equivalent to mature review baselines.

items.text: Create a service principal and its role assignments before creating the ARO clusters.
Use AAD to authenticate users in your ARO cluster.
When using AAD authentication, remove kubeadmin user from the cluster.
Define OpenShift projects to restrict RBAC privilege and isolate workloads in your cluster.
Define the required RBAC roles in OpenShift are scoped to either a project or a cluster.
Minimize the number of users who have administrator rights and secrets access.
Use Privileged Identity Management in AAD for ARO users with privileged roles.
Use Azure DDoS Network/IP Protection to protect the virtual network you use for the ARO cluster unless you use Azure Firewall or WAF in a centralized subscription
All web applications you configure to use an ingress should use TLS encryption and shouldn't allow access over unencrypted HTTP.
Use Azure Front Door with WAF to securely publish ARO applications to the internet, especially in multi-region environments.
If exposing an app on ARO with Azure Front Door, use private link to connect Front Door with the ARO router.
If your security policy requires you to inspect all outbound internet traffic that's generated in the ARO cluster, secure egress network traffic by using Azure Firewall or an NVA.
If your security policy requires you to use a private IP address for the OpenShift API, deploy a private ARO cluster.
Use Azure Private Link to secure network connections to managed Azure services, including to Azure Container Registry.
Establish a monitoring process using the inbuilt Prometheus, OpenShift Logging or Container Insights integration.
Automate the application delivery process through DevOps practices and CI/CD solutions, such as Pipelines/GitOps provided by OpenShift.
Whenever possible, remove the service state from inside containers. Instead, use an Azure platform as a service (PaaS) that supports multiregion replication.
Use RWX storage with inbuilt Azure Files storage class.
Use pod requests and limits to manage the compute resources within a cluster.
Enforce resource quotas on projects.
Define ClusterAutoScaler and MachineAutoScaler to scale machines when your cluster runs out of resources to support more deployments.

Use virtual machine sizes that are large enough to contain multiple container instances so you get the benefits of increased density, but not so large that your cluster can't handle the workload of a failing node.
Deploy machine health checks to automatically repair damaged machines in a machine pool.
Use an alerting system to provide notifications when things need direct action: Container Insights metric alerts or in-built Alerting UI.
Ensure that the cluster is created in a region that supports AZs and create a machine set for each AZ.
Create infrastructure machine sets to hold infrastructure components. Apply specific Kubernetes labels to these machines and then update the infrastructure components to run on only those machines.
Create application backup and plan for restore and include persistent volumes in the backup.
Use pod priorities, so that in case of limited resources the most critical pods will run.
Regulate cluster functions using admission plug-ins, which are commonly used to enforce security policy, resource limitations, or configuration requirements.
Store your container images in Azure Container Registry and geo-replicate the registry to each region.
Optimize the CPU and memory request values, and maximize the efficiency of the cluster resources using vertical pod autoscaler.

Add health probes to your pods to monitor application health. Make sure pods contain livenessProbe and readinessProbe. Use Startup probes to determine the point at which the application has started up.
Scale pods to meet demand using horizontal pod autoscaler.
Use disruption budgets to ensure the required number of pod replicas exist to handle expected application load.
Use pod topology constraints to automatically schedule pods on nodes throughout the cluster.
Leverage Current ARO SLA - 99.95 into BCDR planning
Run user workloads on the worker nodes, not the control plane nodes
Isolate workloads into worker nodes running in individual subnets as needed
items.description: Create infrastructure machine sets to hold infrastructure components. Apply specific Kubernetes labels to these machines and then update the infrastructure components to run on only those machines

items.text: Backup a cluster state for stateful workload scenarios to a paired region
If container storage is required, ensure availability across regions if needed: Using RWX storage with inbuilt Azure Files storage class. Using CSI Drivers for storage provisioning
Whenever possible, move state out of containers and into external databases that support multi-region replication. Avoid Persistent Volumes
Consider blue/green or canary strategies to deploy new releases of application.
Consider using Red Hat OpenShift GitOps. Red Hat OpenShift GitOps uses Argo CD to maintain cluster resources and support application CI/CD.
Keep your clusters on the latest OpenShift version to avoid potential security or upgrade issues.
Connect Azure Red Hat OpenShift clusters to Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes.
For Azure Red Hat OpenShift 4 clusters, etcd data isn't encrypted by default, but it's recommended to enable etcd encryption to provide another layer of data security.
Use Microsoft Defender for Containers supported via Arc-enabled Kubernetes to secure clusters, containers, and applications.
For applications that require access to sensitive information, use a service principal and the AKV Secrets Provider with the extension for Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters.
Secure pod access to resources. Provide the least number of permissions, and avoid using root or privileged escalation.
Monitor and enforce configuration by using the Azure Policy Extension.
Scan your images for vulnerabilities with Microsoft Defender or any other image scanning solution.
Deploy a dedicated and private instance of Azure Container Registry to each landing zone subscription.
